skill.name: s3-upload-handler
skill.description: Guides implementation of AWS S3 file uploads with UI components, client upload helpers, server upload utilities, and presigned URL routes.
skill.summary: Build S3 upload flows with React components, client helpers, server utilities, and presigned URL examples.
skill.author: AayushBaniya2006

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are false positives caused by markdown code spans and fenced examples, and no weak cryptographic algorithm is evidenced in the cited lines. However, the skill includes S3 upload guidance that sets uploaded objects to public-read and builds presigned post policy data from client-provided metadata. This is not confirmed malicious, but it can lead to unintended public file exposure and should be fixed before publication.

high_findings.title: Public S3 ACL Recommended in Upload Policy
high_findings.description: The presigned post example sets acl to public-read for uploaded objects. Using public-read by default can expose user uploads and may bypass bucket-level privacy expectations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The public-read ACL is explicitly shown inside the presigned post Fields object. This is a direct data exposure risk for an upload handler skill.

medium_findings.title: Client-Controlled Metadata Used in Presigned Policy
medium_findings.description: The route reads fileName and fileType from the request and uses them in the S3 key and Content-Type condition. Without server-side validation, users can influence object names and MIME policy decisions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example directly uses values from request.json in Key and Content-Type policy fields. Legitimate use is possible, but missing validation is clear in the snippet.

medium_findings.title: AWS Credential Handling Requires Stronger Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to place AWS access keys in .env.local and shows process.env reads. This is normal for server configuration, but the guidance should require least-privilege credentials and secret manager options.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential references are explicit, but they are part of configuration documentation rather than exfiltration code. Risk depends on how users deploy the generated route.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misread as Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to markdown inline code, fenced code examples, table values, and file path references. No executable Ruby backtick or shell invocation is present in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown documentation and TypeScript or TSX examples. There is no Ruby source or runtime shell execution context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: No Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Found
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings do not correspond to hashing, encryption, or signature code in the cited lines. The content references S3 upload concepts and base64 file content, not a weak cryptographic primitive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm usage. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public S3 ACL Recommended in Upload Policy
dangerous_patterns.description: The presigned post example sets acl to public-read for uploaded objects. Using public-read by default can expose user uploads and may bypass bucket-level privacy expectations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The public-read ACL is explicitly shown inside the presigned post Fields object. This is a direct data exposure risk for an upload handler skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Client-Controlled Metadata Used in Presigned Policy
dangerous_patterns.description: The route reads fileName and fileType from the request and uses them in the S3 key and Content-Type condition. Without server-side validation, users can influence object names and MIME policy decisions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example directly uses values from request.json in Key and Content-Type policy fields. Legitimate use is possible, but missing validation is clear in the snippet.

content.value_statement: Teams often need file uploads that work across React UI, client code, and server routes. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured S3 upload patterns with presigned URL workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use a prebuilt S3Uploader UI component.
Shows how to call a client-side upload helper for custom interfaces.
Describes a server-side upload utility for generated or processed files.
Lists common S3Uploader properties such as maxFiles, maxSize, accept, and multiple.
Provides a presigned post route example using the AWS SDK.
limitations: Does not include the actual component or library source files.
Does not verify bucket policy, CORS, IAM permissions, or deployment settings.
The included route example needs stronger validation and private object defaults.
Assumes a TypeScript and React application structure.

use_cases.title: Add profile image uploads
use_cases.description: Create a user-facing upload button that stores avatar files in S3 through a presigned route.

use_cases.title: Build custom document intake
use_cases.description: Use the client upload helper inside a custom form with controlled file state and upload callbacks.

use_cases.title: Store generated server files
use_cases.description: Upload processed images, reports, or generated PDFs from a backend action to an S3 bucket.

prompt_templates.title: Add a basic uploader
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a standard UI upload flow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the s3-upload-handler skill to add a basic S3Uploader button for one image file. Keep objects private and explain any required route changes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a dropzone flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Multiple file uploads with UI constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the s3-upload-handler skill to design a dropzone upload flow for up to five PDF files. Include size limits, accepted types, and upload success handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review a presigned route
prompt_templates.scenario: Security review for an existing upload endpoint.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the s3-upload-handler skill to review my presigned S3 route. Check ACLs, content type validation, file name handling, expiration, and IAM assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Design a full upload architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced implementation across client and server code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the s3-upload-handler skill to design a complete upload architecture for user documents, including client UI, server validation, private storage, and post-upload processing.

output_examples.input: Add an upload button for user avatars.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan for a single-file S3 upload button, including accepted image types, maximum size, callback behavior, and required presigned route responsibilities.

output_examples.input: Review my S3 upload endpoint before launch.
output: A security checklist covering private ACL defaults, server-side MIME validation, key normalization, expiration limits, and least-privilege IAM.
A prioritized list of changes needed before enabling production uploads.

output_examples.input: Support generated PDF uploads from the server.
output_examples.output: A server-side workflow that prepares generated file content, assigns a safe destination path, sets the correct content type, and returns the stored object URL or key.

best_practices: Use private S3 objects by default and serve files through authorized access paths.
Validate file type, file size, and object key prefixes on the server.
Use least-privilege AWS credentials and rotate them through approved secret management.
anti_patterns: Do not trust client-provided MIME types without server validation.
Do not set public-read ACLs for user uploads unless public access is required and reviewed.
Do not reuse one broad AWS key across development, staging, and production.

faq.question: Does this skill include runnable source code?
faq.answer: No. It provides usage guidance and examples for existing S3 upload components and utilities.

faq.question: Can it help with presigned URL routes?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes a presigned post route pattern and the settings such routes usually need.

faq.question: Is the included route example production-ready?
faq.answer: No. The example should be hardened with private defaults, server validation, and least-privilege IAM.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it manage AWS credentials automatically?
faq.answer: No. Users must configure AWS credentials securely in their own environment or secret manager.

faq.question: What application stack does it assume?
faq.answer: It assumes a TypeScript and React-style project with API routes or server actions.
